QF.id: IV Clearing Settlement and Risk Management_48

question.text: A trader holds 15 lots of long 10-year bond futures and 15 lots of short 5-year bond futures. They claim this is a calendar spread. How would the exchange treat this for margin purposes?

answer.text: This would not qualify as a calendar spread since the underlyings are different bonds; full margin would be charged on both legs
This qualifies as a calendar spread and would receive reduced margin
Only the net position (zero) would be margined
This would receive 50% margin reduction as an inter-commodity spread

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** A calendar spread requires long and short positions in the SAME underlying across different expiry months. Here, 10-year and 5-year bond futures have different underlyings, so it does not qualify as a calendar spread for reduced margin.
**The Trap:** While these positions are hedged in a general sense, the exchange's calendar spread definition requires the same underlying contract. Different tenors are different products.
**Cross-Exam Context:** Calendar spread margin benefits are product-specific across all derivatives. In equity derivatives too, the spread must be in the same underlying stock/index.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** For genuine cross-tenor hedges, traders should explore if the exchange offers any inter-commodity spread margin benefits, though these are typically more limited than calendar spread benefits.
